Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I tried to dedicate around 30 hours a week, but with limited resources and constant anxiety about whether I was doing enough, I'd often end up reading and re-reading my notes late at night. I prefer reading and writing things out, so I'd print materials when I could afford it or copy things into notebooks. The routine was more reactive than planned—I'd study intensely when fear kicked in about falling behind.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how scared I was feeling about my performance. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was paralyzed by anxiety, then I'd cram everything into a few days. Deadlines were my main driver, not any consistent schedule. Being 27 and feeling like I should have my life together by now made the irregular pattern even more stressful.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in—fear of missing something important, fear of failing, fear of being left behind. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that jolt of panic would get me to check. Other times I logged in just to reassure myself that I hadn't missed anything critical, even if I didn't actually do much once I was there.
Q4: Not really, if I'm honest. The LMS never became a comfortable routine for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, but then life would get overwhelming and I'd avoid it for days. The few times it felt routine were right before major deadlines when I had no choice but to be there constantly.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and study them on my own time, annotating and making notes. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students asked questions I was too anxious to ask myself. Video lectures were okay but I found myself getting distracted.
Q6: I often ignored the discussion posts and peer interaction features. With my anxiety, putting myself out there felt terrifying—what if I said something stupid? I also skipped a lot of optional videos because watching 156 minutes of content online when you have limited resources and high stress just felt overwhelming. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later rarely came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exams or assignments. With everything going on in my head and my limited resources, I couldn't afford to spend time on things that seemed peripheral. If I could see a clear line between the activity and my grade, I'd prioritize it. The CBT sessions I've been doing helped me recognize this pattern.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, mentally or time-wise. Required ones came with that built-in fear of consequences, which paradoxically made them easier to approach. Optional stuff just added to my anxiety—another thing I 'should' be doing but probably wouldn't get to. I'd feel guilty about skipping them, which didn't help my stress levels.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded materials, handwritten notes, and sometimes borrowed textbooks. I'd read and rewrite concepts multiple times—that's genuinely how I learn. The LMS was more like a portal to get materials rather than where the real learning happened. My notebooks are filled with course content that would never show up in any log.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through problems on paper or reading printed PDFs at the library where internet wasn't needed. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person, working through concepts together. None of that showed up anywhere. Some of my best understanding came from those offline sessions, but to the system, it probably looked like I wasn't doing anything.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download everything I could in one session—PDFs, lecture slides, any documents available—and then study them offline for days or weeks. With my internet access being what it is and my preference for reading and writing, this was just practical. One login might fuel a week of actual studying that the system would never see.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods when my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't face the course. Around midterms, I remember feeling so scared about my performance that I'd avoid the LMS entirely because logging in meant confronting how behind I was. The fear became paralyzing. My attendance dropped because showing up meant admitting I was struggling.
Q13: All of those affected me significantly. Being 27 with low resources means I have responsibilities beyond just studying—work, family expectations, financial stress. When those piled up, the LMS became just another source of anxiety. Fatigue from working and studying 30 hours a week made it hard to engage meaningfully. Sometimes I'd log in exhausted and just stare at the screen.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos or readings and my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about assignments, about my future, about whether I was good enough. My eyes would scan the words but nothing would stick. I'd have to go back and re-read everything later when I was calmer. The LMS would show I accessed something, but real engagement? That was rare in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—what if I did it wrong? What if it wasn't good enough? So I'd avoid it until the deadline forced my hand. This explains my low completion rate despite actually caring about the course. The CBT sessions have been helping me recognize this avoidance pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They'd trigger intense anxiety spikes, but they were also the only thing that could break through my avoidance. I'd cram everything into the last possible moment, which meant my LMS activity probably looked very spiky—nothing for days, then frantic activity right before due dates.
Q17: Feedback affected me deeply, sometimes too deeply. Good feedback would motivate me for a while, but critical feedback would send me spiraling. I'd avoid checking grades sometimes because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did get my assignment scores back and they were decent, it helped, but the fear of the next one would immediately set in.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with five online courses running simultaneously. Having so many things to track increased my anxiety significantly. I needed more guidance on prioritization, but instead felt lost in a sea of tasks. The structure assumed a level of organization and mental clarity I often didn't have.
Q19: The flexibility was both good and bad—good because I could work around my schedule, bad because without structure, my anxiety would fill the void with worst-case scenarios. Clearer weekly checklists would have helped. The discussion requirements felt forced and added stress rather than value. Having materials downloadable was genuinely helpful for my learning style.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking through things in a panic, but being too overwhelmed to actually complete and submit work. High activity might reflect worry and checking rather than productive engagement. Fear can make you very active without being very effective.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My exam score was decent and I got Merit despite low LMS engagement because my real learning happened offline. I'd read and write extensively outside the system, really engaging with the material in ways that worked for my brain. The LMS numbers don't capture those hours with my notebooks.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, submission times. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline work, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone's anxiously refreshing versus genuinely learning. They miss the fear, the avoidance, the real human struggle behind the data points.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just going through the motions. It means caring about understanding, not just passing. For me, real engagement often looks like sitting quietly with my notes, writing and rewriting concepts until they click. It's mental presence, not just physical or digital presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my constant anxiety, the hours I spent studying offline, the peer study sessions, or how much the CBT sessions helped me manage my fear enough to actually perform. It wouldn't show that behind my low engagement numbers is someone who genuinely cares about learning but struggles with the psychological barriers to showing up. It wouldn't show that I'm scared, but I'm trying.
